---
title: "Reading the Surgeons' Own Numbers"
subtitle: "Methodological naïveté in the intersex genitoplasty outcomes literature"
date: 2026-06-21
categories: [intersex]
draft: true
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
---

A useful way to assess a clinical specialty is not to ask what it claims but to read its
own outcome papers and notice what they count as success, who does the counting, and when.
On that test, the surgical literature on feminizing genitoplasty for intersex / DSD
conditions—published overwhelmingly in urology journals—does not read as the work of
authors fluent in research design or statistics. The problem is not a scattering of weak
papers; it is that the field's standard way of measuring "success" is built on a series of
methodological choices that would make a first-year epidemiology student laugh.

A single paper makes the point cleanly.

## A representative specimen

Gosalbez and colleagues, in the *Journal of Urology*, asked in their title whether an
established surgical technique was now "obsolete."[^gosalbez] The evidence offered for that
comparative claim was a retrospective series of **eleven children**, all operated on by a
**single surgeon**, with follow-up running from three months to five years (mean 2.5).
Children were operated on as young as three months of age. The headline result was that the
cosmetic appearance was, in the authors' phrasing, "superior to that achieved with previous
techniques"—a judgment rendered by the operating surgeon, on his own work, with no control
group, no blinding, no validated instrument, and no patient-reported measure.

Every element of that study is broken in a recognizable way:

- **The outcome is mis-defined.** The endpoint is the *appearance* of the genitals, not the
  function of the adult who will inhabit them. Sexual sensation, orgasmic capacity, comfort
  with penetration, the need for further surgery, and—above all—the patient's own
  retrospective judgment are the outcomes that matter, and none can be observed in a
  three-year-old.
- **The assessor is conflicted and unblinded.** The surgeon grades his own cosmetic result.
  There is no independent examiner and no role for the patient.
- **The design cannot support the claim.** A descriptive, uncontrolled, single-surgeon case
  series of eleven is being used to make a *comparative superiority* claim against an
  established technique. That is a category error, not a small one.
- **The timing guarantees the wrong answer.** Follow-up ends before puberty and long before
  sexual debut, so the study is structurally incapable of observing its own most important
  endpoints. The authors acknowledge the need for longer follow-up in a sentence—and publish
  the superiority claim anyway.

This is not an outlier dredged up to be unfair. It ran in the flagship journal of the
specialty, and the pattern it exemplifies persists.

## The pattern persists, with statistics added

A more recent, more ambitious effort—an eleven-site prospective study by Bernabé and
colleagues, again in a urology journal—shows the same conceptual frame dressed in slightly
more methodological apparatus.[^bernabe] Across all sites the cohort was **27 children**.
The primary cosmetic outcome was, once again, appearance at 12 months, "as rated by surgeons
and parents." The patient—the one person who will live in the body and is the only valid
judge of most of what matters—does not appear as an assessor.

The statistical handling is its own tell. Genital appearance was scored on a **4-point
Likert scale**, and the investigators then applied **paired *t*-tests** to those ratings to
show that surgeons and parents agreed. Applying an interval-scale, parametric test to a
4-point ordinal scale, in *n* = 27, to demonstrate agreement between the two
non-independent parties least able to assess the outcome, is a compact illustration of the
whole problem: the wrong test, on the wrong scale, answering the wrong question.

## The contrast that proves it was a choice

The decisive point is that none of this was unavoidable. In the same era and the same
patient population, a different group of investigators—largely gynecologists and the
Creighton–Liao–Minto collaboration, with at least one urology group joining—did the
straightforward things the surgical series omit: define a real outcome, wait until it can be
observed, and use independent or patient-reported assessment.

When they did, the picture inverted. Alizai and colleagues examined girls *after* puberty
and found clitoral surgical outcomes unsatisfactory in a substantial fraction, including in
cases operated by named specialist pediatric urologists, with most patients needing further
vaginal surgery for comfortable intercourse.[^alizai] Creighton, Minto, and Steele, using
objective anatomical and cosmetic assessment at adolescence, found cosmetic results poor in
roughly two-fifths of patients and a near-universal requirement for further
treatment.[^creighton2001] Minto and colleagues went further still, running a cross-sectional
study *with a comparison group* and validated sexual-function measures, and found that
clitoral surgery was associated with worse sexual outcomes.[^minto2003] Crouch and colleagues
documented reduced genital sensation on objective testing.[^crouch2008]

Same operations, same diagnoses, same decades. The only variable that changed was the
quality of the method—and with it, the conclusion. That is what makes the naïveté
diagnosable rather than merely unfortunate: the tools to do it properly existed and were in
use down the hall. The surgical-outcomes literature largely did not reach for them.

## The strongest defense—and why it falls short

The fair counterargument is that 12-month cosmesis is a legitimate *early* safety and
feasibility endpoint; that long-term follow-up in rare, geographically scattered conditions
is genuinely hard; and that authors label these reports "preliminary." All true. But a
preliminary safety report does not license a title asking whether a rival technique is
"obsolete," nor a literature that has, for decades, allowed infant cosmetic appearance to
stand in for the success of an irreversible operation whose stakes are adult sexual function
and bodily self-determination. The difficulty of long-term follow-up is an argument for
*caution about operating*, not for measuring the easy thing and calling it an outcome.

## Why the method is not separable from the ethics

The recurring choice—surgeon-judged appearance, in childhood, without the patient—encodes an
assumption about what the surgery is *for*: making genitals look acceptable to adults, rather
than serving the future adult's function and consent. The methodological weakness and the
ethical objection are the same fact seen from two sides. A field that had defined its
outcomes around the patient could not have measured success this way; a field that measures
success this way has already decided whose judgment counts.

## At a glance

| Study | Journal | Design / *n* | Who judged | When | "Success" defined as | Finding |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gosalbez 2005[^gosalbez] | J Urol | Retrospective case series, single surgeon, *n* = 11 | Operating surgeon | 3 mo–5 yr (mean 2.5) | Cosmetic appearance | "Superior" to prior technique |
| Bernabé 2018[^bernabe] | J Pediatr Urol | 11-site prospective, *n* = 27 | Surgeons + parents | 12 months | Cosmetic appearance (4-pt Likert) | Surgeons & parents "satisfied" |
| Alizai 1999[^alizai] | J Urol | Examination under anesthesia, *n* = 14 | Multidisciplinary team | After puberty | Anatomic + functional adequacy | Frequently unsatisfactory; reoperation usual |
| Creighton 2001[^creighton2001] | Lancet | Objective outcome study, *n* = 44 | Independent assessment | Adolescence | Objective cosmesis + anatomy | Poor in ~41%; further surgery near-universal |
| Minto 2003[^minto2003] | Lancet | Cross-sectional, **with comparison group**, validated measures | Patient-reported | Adulthood | Sexual function | Clitoral surgery → worse outcomes |
| Crouch 2008[^crouch2008] | J Urol | Objective sensory testing | Independent + patient | Adulthood | Genital sensation | Reduced sensitivity |

[^gosalbez]: Gosalbez R, Castellan M, Ibrahim E, DiSandro M, Labbie A. New concepts in feminizing genitoplasty—is the Fortunoff flap obsolete? *J Urol.* 2005;174(6):2350–2353; discussion 2353. PMID 16280841. doi:10.1097/01.ju.0000180419.62193.78

[^bernabe]: Bernabé KJ, Nokoff NJ, Galan D, Felsen D, Aston CE, Austin P, Baskin L, Chan YM, Cheng EY, Diamond DA, et al. Preliminary report: surgical outcomes following genitoplasty in children with moderate to severe genital atypia. *J Pediatr Urol.* 2018;14(2):157.e1–157.e8. PMID 29398588. doi:10.1016/j.jpurol.2017.11.019

[^alizai]: Alizai NK, Thomas DF, Lilford RJ, Batchelor AG, Johnson N. Feminizing genitoplasty for congenital adrenal hyperplasia: what happens at puberty? *J Urol.* 1999;161(5):1588–1591. PMID 10210421

[^creighton2001]: Creighton SM, Minto CL, Steele SJ. Objective cosmetic and anatomical outcomes at adolescence of feminising surgery for ambiguous genitalia done in childhood. *Lancet.* 2001;358(9276):124–125. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(01)05343-0

[^minto2003]: Minto CL, Liao LM, Woodhouse CRJ, Ransley PG, Creighton SM. The effect of clitoral surgery on sexual outcome in individuals who have intersex conditions with ambiguous genitalia: a cross-sectional study. *Lancet.* 2003;361(9365):1252–1257

[^crouch2008]: Crouch NS, Liao LM, Woodhouse CRJ, Conway GS, Creighton SM. Sexual function and genital sensitivity following feminizing genitoplasty for congenital adrenal hyperplasia. *J Urol.* 2008;179(2):634–638
